speaker: OSWALD
text: Good dawning to thee, friend. Art of this

speaker: OSWALD
text: house?

speaker: KENT
text: Ay.

speaker: OSWALD
text: Where may we set our horses?

speaker: KENT
text: I’ th’ mire.

speaker: OSWALD
text: Prithee, if thou lov’st me, tell me.

speaker: KENT
text: I love thee not.

speaker: OSWALD
text: Why then, I care not for thee.

speaker: KENT
text: If I had thee in Lipsbury pinfold, I would make

speaker: KENT
text: thee care for me.

speaker: OSWALD
text: Why dost thou use me thus? I know thee not.

speaker: KENT
text: Fellow, I know thee.

speaker: OSWALD
text: What dost thou know me for?

speaker: KENT
text: A knave, a rascal, an eater of broken meats; a

speaker: KENT
text: base, proud, shallow, beggarly, three-suited, hundred-pound,

speaker: KENT
text: filthy worsted-stocking knave; a lily-livered,

speaker: KENT
text: action-taking, whoreson, glass-gazing, superserviceable,

speaker: KENT
text: finical rogue; one-trunk-inheriting

speaker: KENT
text: slave; one that wouldst be a bawd in way of good

speaker: KENT
text: service, and art nothing but the composition of a

speaker: KENT
text: knave, beggar, coward, pander, and the son and heir

speaker: KENT
text: of a mongrel bitch; one whom I will beat into

speaker: KENT
text: clamorous whining if thou deny’st the least syllable

speaker: KENT
text: of thy addition.

speaker: OSWALD
text: Why, what a monstrous fellow art thou thus

speaker: OSWALD
text: to rail on one that is neither known of thee nor

speaker: OSWALD
text: knows thee!

speaker: KENT
text: What a brazen-faced varlet art thou to deny thou

speaker: KENT
text: knowest me! Is it two days ago since I tripped up

speaker: KENT
text: thy heels and beat thee before the King?

speaker: KENT
text: Draw, you rogue, for though it be night,

speaker: KENT
text: yet the moon shines. I’ll make a sop o’ th’ moonshine

speaker: KENT
text: of you, you whoreson, cullionly barbermonger.

speaker: KENT
text: Draw!

speaker: OSWALD
text: Away! I have nothing to do with thee.

speaker: KENT
text: Draw, you rascal! You come with letters against

speaker: KENT
text: the King and take Vanity the puppet’s part against

speaker: KENT
text: the royalty of her father. Draw, you rogue, or I’ll so

speaker: KENT
text: carbonado your shanks! Draw, you rascal! Come

speaker: KENT
text: your ways.

speaker: OSWALD
text: Help, ho! Murder! Help!

speaker: KENT
text: Strike, you slave! Stand, rogue! Stand, you neat

speaker: KENT
text: slave! Strike!

speaker: OSWALD
text: Help, ho! Murder, murder!

speaker: EDMUND
text: How now, what’s the matter? Part!

speaker: KENT
text: With you, goodman boy, if you please. Come, I’ll

speaker: KENT
text: flesh you. Come on, young master.

speaker: GLOUCESTER
text: Weapons? Arms? What’s the matter here?

speaker: CORNWALL
text: Keep peace, upon your lives! He dies that

speaker: CORNWALL
text: strikes again. What is the matter?

speaker: REGAN
text: The messengers from our sister and the King.

speaker: CORNWALL
text: What is your difference? Speak.

speaker: OSWALD
text: I am scarce in breath, my lord.

speaker: KENT
text: No marvel, you have so bestirred your valor.

speaker: KENT
text: You cowardly rascal, nature disclaims in thee; a

speaker: KENT
text: tailor made thee.

speaker: CORNWALL
text: Thou art a strange fellow. A tailor make a

speaker: CORNWALL
text: man?

speaker: KENT
text: A tailor, sir. A stonecutter or a painter could not

speaker: KENT
text: have made him so ill, though they had been but two

speaker: KENT
text: years o’ th’ trade.

speaker: CORNWALL
text: Speak yet, how grew your quarrel?

speaker: OSWALD
text: This ancient ruffian, sir, whose life I have

speaker: OSWALD
text: spared at suit of his gray beard—

speaker: KENT
text: Thou whoreson zed, thou unnecessary letter!

speaker: KENT
text: —My lord, if you will give me leave, I will tread

speaker: KENT
text: this unbolted villain into mortar and daub the wall

speaker: KENT
text: of a jakes with him.—Spare my gray beard, you

speaker: KENT
text: wagtail?

speaker: CORNWALL
text: Peace, sirrah!

speaker: CORNWALL
text: You beastly knave, know you no reverence?

speaker: KENT
text: Yes, sir, but anger hath a privilege.

speaker: CORNWALL
text: Why art thou angry?

speaker: KENT
text: That such a slave as this should wear a sword,

speaker: KENT
text: Who wears no honesty. Such smiling rogues as these,

speaker: KENT
text: Like rats, oft bite the holy cords atwain

speaker: KENT
text: Which are too intrinse t’ unloose; smooth every passion

speaker: KENT
text: That in the natures of their lords rebel—

speaker: KENT
text: Being oil to fire, snow to the colder moods—

speaker: KENT
text: Renege, affirm, and turn their halcyon beaks

speaker: KENT
text: With every gale and vary of their masters,

speaker: KENT
text: Knowing naught, like dogs, but following.—

speaker: KENT
text: A plague upon your epileptic visage!

speaker: KENT
text: Smile you my speeches, as I were a fool?

speaker: KENT
text: Goose, if I had you upon Sarum plain,

speaker: KENT
text: I’d drive you cackling home to Camelot.

speaker: CORNWALL
text: What, art thou mad, old fellow?

speaker: GLOUCESTER
text: How fell you out? Say that.

speaker: KENT
text: No contraries hold more antipathy

speaker: KENT
text: Than I and such a knave.

speaker: CORNWALL
text: Why dost thou call him “knave”? What is his fault?

speaker: KENT
text: His countenance likes me not.

speaker: CORNWALL
text: No more, perchance, does mine, nor his, nor hers.

speaker: KENT
text: Sir, ’tis my occupation to be plain:

speaker: KENT
text: I have seen better faces in my time

speaker: KENT
text: Than stands on any shoulder that I see

speaker: KENT
text: Before me at this instant.

speaker: CORNWALL
text: This is some fellow

speaker: CORNWALL
text: Who, having been praised for bluntness, doth affect

speaker: CORNWALL
text: A saucy roughness and constrains the garb

speaker: CORNWALL
text: Quite from his nature. He cannot flatter, he.

speaker: CORNWALL
text: An honest mind and plain, he must speak truth!

speaker: CORNWALL
text: An they will take it, so; if not, he’s plain.

speaker: CORNWALL
text: These kind of knaves I know, which in this plainness

speaker: CORNWALL
text: Harbor more craft and more corrupter ends

speaker: CORNWALL
text: Than twenty silly-ducking observants

speaker: CORNWALL
text: That stretch their duties nicely.

speaker: KENT
text: Sir, in good faith, in sincere verity,

speaker: KENT
text: Under th’ allowance of your great aspect,

speaker: KENT
text: Whose influence, like the wreath of radiant fire

speaker: KENT
text: On flick’ring Phoebus’ front—

speaker: CORNWALL
text: What mean’st by this?

speaker: KENT
text: To go out of my dialect, which you discommend

speaker: KENT
text: so much. I know, sir, I am no flatterer. He that

speaker: KENT
text: beguiled you in a plain accent was a plain knave,

speaker: KENT
text: which for my part I will not be, though I should

speaker: KENT
text: win your displeasure to entreat me to ’t.

speaker: CORNWALL
text: What was th’ offense you gave

speaker: CORNWALL
text: him?

speaker: OSWALD
text: I never gave him any.

speaker: OSWALD
text: It pleased the King his master very late

speaker: OSWALD
text: To strike at me, upon his misconstruction;

speaker: OSWALD
text: When he, compact, and flattering his displeasure,

speaker: OSWALD
text: Tripped me behind; being down, insulted, railed,

speaker: OSWALD
text: And put upon him such a deal of man

speaker: OSWALD
text: That worthied him, got praises of the King

speaker: OSWALD
text: For him attempting who was self-subdued;

speaker: OSWALD
text: And in the fleshment of this dread exploit,

speaker: OSWALD
text: Drew on me here again.

speaker: KENT
text: None of these rogues and cowards

speaker: KENT
text: But Ajax is their fool.

speaker: CORNWALL
text: Fetch forth the stocks.—

speaker: CORNWALL
text: You stubborn ancient knave, you reverent braggart,

speaker: CORNWALL
text: We’ll teach you.

speaker: KENT
text: Sir, I am too old to learn.

speaker: KENT
text: Call not your stocks for me. I serve the King,

speaker: KENT
text: On whose employment I was sent to you.

speaker: KENT
text: You shall do small respect, show too bold malice

speaker: KENT
text: Against the grace and person of my master,

speaker: KENT
text: Stocking his messenger.

speaker: CORNWALL
text: Fetch forth the stocks.—As I have life and honor,

speaker: CORNWALL
text: There shall he sit till noon.

speaker: REGAN
text: Till noon? Till night, my lord, and all night, too.

speaker: KENT
text: Why, madam, if I were your father’s dog,

speaker: KENT
text: You should not use me so.

speaker: REGAN
text: Sir, being his knave, I will.

speaker: CORNWALL
text: This is a fellow of the selfsame color

speaker: CORNWALL
text: Our sister speaks of.—Come, bring away the stocks.

speaker: GLOUCESTER
text: Let me beseech your Grace not to do so.

speaker: GLOUCESTER
text: His fault is much, and the good king his master

speaker: GLOUCESTER
text: Will check him for ’t. Your purposed low correction

speaker: GLOUCESTER
text: Is such as basest and contemned’st wretches

speaker: GLOUCESTER
text: For pilf’rings and most common trespasses

speaker: GLOUCESTER
text: Are punished with. The King must take it ill

speaker: GLOUCESTER
text: That he, so slightly valued in his messenger,

speaker: GLOUCESTER
text: Should have him thus restrained.

speaker: CORNWALL
text: I’ll answer that.

speaker: REGAN
text: My sister may receive it much more worse

speaker: REGAN
text: To have her gentleman abused, assaulted

speaker: REGAN
text: For following her affairs.—Put in his legs.

speaker: CORNWALL
text: Come, my good lord, away.

speaker: GLOUCESTER
text: I am sorry for thee, friend. ’Tis the Duke’s pleasure,

speaker: GLOUCESTER
text: Whose disposition all the world well knows

speaker: GLOUCESTER
text: Will not be rubbed nor stopped. I’ll entreat for thee.

speaker: KENT
text: Pray, do not, sir. I have watched and traveled hard.

speaker: KENT
text: Some time I shall sleep out; the rest I’ll whistle.

speaker: KENT
text: A good man’s fortune may grow out at heels.

speaker: KENT
text: Give you good morrow.

speaker: GLOUCESTER
text: The Duke’s to blame in this. ’Twill be ill taken.

speaker: KENT
text: Good king, that must approve the common saw,

speaker: KENT
text: Thou out of heaven’s benediction com’st

speaker: KENT
text: To the warm sun.

speaker: KENT
text: Approach, thou beacon to this under globe,

speaker: KENT
text: That by thy comfortable beams I may

speaker: KENT
text: Peruse this letter. Nothing almost sees miracles

speaker: KENT
text: But misery. I know ’tis from Cordelia,

speaker: KENT
text: Who hath most fortunately been informed

speaker: KENT
text: Of my obscurèd course, and shall find time

speaker: KENT
text: From this enormous state, seeking to give

speaker: KENT
text: Losses their remedies. All weary and o’erwatched,

speaker: KENT
text: Take vantage, heavy eyes, not to behold

speaker: KENT
text: This shameful lodging.

speaker: KENT
text: Fortune, good night. Smile once more; turn thy wheel.
